title: Making clay slip and slurry
subtitle: The two liquid clay mixtures hand-builders rely on for joining and decorating
excerpt: Slip and slurry are both clay dissolved in water, but they serve different jobs. Slurry is a thick paste used to join clay pieces together so the bond holds through drying. Slip is thinner, brushable, and applied for decorative surface coats. Both are made from the same clay body you are using for the project.
sourceNotes: Slip and slurry preparation is a fundamental pottery joining technique. Encyclopaedia Britannica, 11th edition (1911), ceramics entry. Technique derived from first principles.

content.text: is consistency, and consistency determines function. Slurry joins clay to clay; slip paints surfaces. Both are made from the offcuts and scraps of the clay body you are already working with, so they match the body exactly in shrinkage rate.

Collect 20-30 g of clay scraps from the project. Drop them into a small jar or container. Add water a teaspoon at a time, working the scraps with a finger until the mixture reaches the consistency of thick yoghurt. You want it spreadable but not runny. A slurry that flows off a brush is too thin for joining; it won't hold the pieces together as the clay shrinks during drying.

Use the same process but add more water until the mixture runs freely off a dipped finger, similar to single cream. Coloured slips are made by mixing a small amount of acrylic paint or iron oxide (from a pottery supplier) into the base slip before brushing. Keep decorative slip in a sealed jar; it separates on standing and needs stirring before each use.
Using slurry to join two clay pieces

. Score both joining surfaces with a needle tool or fork, making a crosshatch of shallow scratches about 2 mm apart. Brush slurry onto both scored faces. Press the pieces together firmly and hold for 20-30 seconds. Wipe away the slurry that squeezes out from the join with a damp sponge.
What it looks like when it is right

A properly joined seam shows a thin bead of slurry along its full length after pressing. The join feels solid to a light press with a thumb and does not spring open. Once the join dries to leather-hard, running a wet finger along the seam blends it into the surrounding surface.
Joining without scoring. Wet clay pressed onto wet clay without roughening the surfaces often separates as it dries. The scored texture gives the slurry something to lock into.

Joining bone-dry clay to wet clay. The shrinkage rates differ. Wet clay shrinks as it dries; bone-dry clay does not. The stress at the join pulls the pieces apart. Join at the same moisture stage, ideally both at soft or both at leather-hard.
Slurry too thin. If it runs freely off the brush it will not build up on the join and will dry before the pieces are pressed together.

items.fix: Rebind the crack with fresh slurry and a thin strip of paper clay pressed into the seam; paper clay bridges crack gaps better than plain air-dry clay
items.cause: Pieces were at different drying stages when joined, or the slurry was too wet
items.symptom: Join cracks as the piece dries

items.fix: Separate the pieces carefully, score and slurry properly, and re-join
items.cause: Surfaces were not scored and slurried before pressing
items.symptom: Pieces come apart immediately after joining

items.fix: Seal with a lid and a small square of damp cloth on the surface of the slip; slurry keeps for weeks this way
items.symptom: Slurry dries out in the jar between sessions
